questions.q: A morphism of schemes $f\colon X\to Y$ is a continuous map $|X|\to|Y|$ together with a map of sheaves of rings $f^{\#}\colon \mathcal{O}_Y\to f_{*}\mathcal{O}_X$ satisfying which extra condition?
choices: the induced map on stalks $f^{\#}_x\colon \mathcal{O}_{Y,f(x)}\to \mathcal{O}_{X,x}$ is a local homomorphism (sends maximal ideal into maximal ideal)
$f$ is a homeomorphism onto its image
$f^{\#}$ is surjective on every open set
questions.explain: A morphism of schemes is a morphism of locally ringed spaces: the stalk maps $f^{\#}_x$ must be local, i.e. send the maximal ideal $\mathfrak{m}_{f(x)}$ into $\mathfrak{m}_x$. Without this, arbitrary ringed-space maps between spectra don't correspond to ring maps.

questions.q: For affine schemes, what is the anti-equivalence between rings and affine schemes?
choices: Ring homomorphisms $A\to B$ correspond to scheme morphisms $\operatorname{Spec} A\to \operatorname{Spec} B$ (same direction)
Ring homomorphisms $A\to B$ correspond to scheme morphisms $\operatorname{Spec} B\to \operatorname{Spec} A$ (reversed direction)
Ring homomorphisms correspond to continuous maps only, not scheme morphisms
Every continuous map of spectra comes from a unique ring map
questions.explain: $\operatorname{Spec}$ is a contravariant functor: a ring map $\varphi\colon A\to B$ induces $\operatorname{Spec}\varphi\colon \operatorname{Spec} B\to\operatorname{Spec} A$ by pulling back prime ideals via $\mathfrak{q}\mapsto \varphi^{-1}(\mathfrak{q})$.

questions.q: How many morphisms $\operatorname{Spec}\mathbb{F}_5\to \operatorname{Spec}\mathbb{Z}[x]/(x^2-1)$ over $\operatorname{Spec}\mathbb{Z}$ are there? (Equivalently, how many ring maps $\mathbb{Z}[x]/(x^2-1)\to \mathbb{F}_5$ fixing $\mathbb{Z}$?)
questions.explain: Such a ring map is determined by the image of $x$, which must satisfy $x^2=1$ in $\mathbb{F}_5$. The solutions are $x=\pm 1$, so there are $2$ morphisms.

questions.q: A morphism $i\colon Z\to X$ is a closed immersion iff:
choices: $i$ is a homeomorphism onto a closed subset of $X$ and $i^{\#}\colon \mathcal{O}_X\to i_{*}\mathcal{O}_Z$ is surjective
questions.explain: Closed immersion = topological closed inclusion + surjective map of structure sheaves. The kernel of $i^{\#}$ is the ideal sheaf cutting out $Z$.

questions.q: Which ring map corresponds to the open immersion $D(f)\hookrightarrow \operatorname{Spec} A$?
questions.explain: Open immersions onto a basic open come from localization: $A\to A_f = A[1/f]$ inverts $f$, and primes of $A_f$ correspond exactly to primes of $A$ not containing $f$.

questions.q: The closed subscheme $V((x^2))\hookrightarrow \operatorname{Spec} k[x]$ has the same underlying topological space as $V((x))$ (just the origin), but a different structure sheaf. What is $\dim_k \Gamma(V((x^2)),\mathcal{O}) = \dim_k k[x]/(x^2)$?
questions.explain: $k[x]/(x^2)$ has $k$-basis $\{1,x\}$, so dimension $2$. This is the 'fat point' / tangent direction attached to the origin — a non-reduced closed subscheme.

questions.q: In the category of affine schemes, the fiber product $\operatorname{Spec} A \times_{\operatorname{Spec} R} \operatorname{Spec} B$ is represented by which ring?
questions.explain: Fiber product is the categorical pullback; on affines it's dual to the pushout in rings, which is the tensor product: $\operatorname{Spec} A\times_{\operatorname{Spec} R}\operatorname{Spec} B = \operatorname{Spec}(A\otimes_R B)$.

questions.q: Which property is preserved under arbitrary base change?
choices: being a bijection on underlying topological spaces
being surjective on closed points of a specific field
having exactly one point in every fiber
questions.explain: Closed immersions are preserved by base change: if $Z\hookrightarrow X$ is closed and $X'\to X$ any morphism, then $Z\times_X X'\hookrightarrow X'$ is again a closed immersion. Topological bijectivity and point-count in fibers are not stable under base change.

questions.q: As a $\mathbb{Q}$-vector space, what is the dimension of $\mathbb{Q}(\sqrt{2})\otimes_{\mathbb{Q}} \mathbb{Q}(\sqrt{2})$?
questions.explain: $\mathbb{Q}(\sqrt 2)$ has $\mathbb{Q}$-dimension $2$, so the tensor product has dimension $2\cdot 2 = 4$. Geometrically, $\operatorname{Spec}\mathbb{Q}(\sqrt 2)\times_{\operatorname{Spec}\mathbb{Q}}\operatorname{Spec}\mathbb{Q}(\sqrt 2)$ is $4$-dimensional over $\mathbb{Q}$ and splits as two points over $\mathbb{Q}(\sqrt 2)$.

questions.q: For a morphism $f\colon X\to Y$ and a point $y\in Y$ with residue field $\kappa(y)$, the scheme-theoretic fiber $X_y$ is defined as:
questions.explain: The scheme-theoretic fiber is the base change $X\times_Y \operatorname{Spec}\kappa(y)$. Its underlying topological space is $f^{-1}(y)$, but the scheme structure records multiplicities and nilpotents.

questions.q: Consider $f\colon \operatorname{Spec}\mathbb{Z}[x]/(x^2-5)\to \operatorname{Spec}\mathbb{Z}$. How many (geometric, i.e. over $\overline{\mathbb{F}_p}$) points does the fiber above the closed point $(5)\in\operatorname{Spec}\mathbb{Z}$ have, counted without multiplicity?
questions.explain: The fiber over $(5)$ is $\operatorname{Spec}\mathbb{F}_5[x]/(x^2)$, a single point with a nilpotent — ramified. Topologically the fiber has $1$ point; scheme-theoretically its length is $2$.

questions.q: For the same family $f\colon \operatorname{Spec}\mathbb{Z}[x]/(x^2-5)\to \operatorname{Spec}\mathbb{Z}$, how many geometric points does the fiber over $(11)$ have? (Hint: is $5$ a square mod $11$? Check $4^2=16\equiv 5$.)
questions.explain: Since $5\equiv 4^2\pmod{11}$, the polynomial $x^2-5$ factors as $(x-4)(x+4)$ over $\mathbb{F}_{11}$, so the fiber is $\operatorname{Spec}\mathbb{F}_{11}[x]/(x-4)(x+4)$, which has $2$ reduced points. The prime $11$ splits in $\mathbb{Z}[\sqrt 5]$.

questions.q: A morphism $f\colon X\to S$ is separated iff the diagonal $\Delta_f\colon X\to X\times_S X$ is:
questions.explain: Separatedness = closed diagonal. This is the scheme-theoretic analog of Hausdorff, where in topology the diagonal is closed iff limits are unique.

questions.q: Which of the following schemes over $\operatorname{Spec} k$ fails to be separated?
choices: the affine line with doubled origin (two copies of $\mathbb{A}^1_k$ glued along $\mathbb{A}^1_k\setminus\{0\}$)
questions.explain: The line with doubled origin is the canonical non-separated scheme: both origins are limits of the same punctured line, so the diagonal of the structure map is not closed. All other options are separated.

questions.q: Which of these morphisms is proper?
questions.explain: Projective space is the prototype proper morphism over a field. Affine space fails the valuative criterion (a $K$-point like $1/t\in\mathbb{A}^1_K$ does not extend to the DVR), as does $\mathbb{G}_m$.
